summary: DeVault argues that web browsers have grown so complex through perpetual feature competition that it is now impossible to build a new one from scratch. He quantifies this by showing the W3C specification catalogue totals 114 million words, exceeding the combined word counts of major computing standards, all published RFCs, and the longest novels ever written. This complexity makes browsers expensive to run, riddled with security vulnerabilities, and impossible to fork or compete with. The resulting browser duopoly allows Google and Mozilla to act against users' interests with tracking, ads, and anti-technologies like DRM. He concludes that browser competition should have focused on performance and stability rather than endless new features.
key_points: The W3C specification catalogue totals 114 million words, dwarfing the combined complexity of C11, C++17, UEFI, USB 3.2, POSIX, all published RFCs, and the longest novels ever written
Building a new browser engine is comparable in effort to the Apollo program or the Manhattan project, making competition effectively impossible
The browser duopoly exploits this barrier to act against users — Firefox fills with ads and tracking, Chrome serves Google's surveillance and pushes DRM and AMP
Web browsers are responsible for over 8,000 CVEs, a direct consequence of their obscene complexity
W3C specifications grow by about 200 new specs per year, making it impossible for even a fork to keep pace
money_quotes: The total word count of the W3C specification catalogue is 114 million words at the time of writing.
I conclude that it is impossible to build a new web browser. The complexity of the web is obscene.
The creation of a new web browser would be comparable in effort to the Apollo program or the Manhattan project.
Web browsers are responsible for more than 8,000 CVEs.
Browsers have also been free to stop being the "user agent" and start being the agents of their creators instead.

Firefox is filling up with ads, tracking, and mandatory plugins. Chrome is used as a means for Google to efficiently track your eyeballs and muscle anti-technologies like DRM and AMP into the ecosystem.
This strategy of unlimited scope and perpetual feature creep is reckless, and has been allowed to go on for far too long.
The browser wars have been allowed to continue for far too long. They should have long ago focused on competing in terms of performance and stability, not in adding new web "features".
key_insight: The unchecked growth of web standards has made browsers so complex that no new competitor can emerge, creating an unaccountable duopoly that serves corporate interests over users.
